module_mood_contract: Satisfied compounding — 'it runs while I sleep.' Persistence + automation turn a one-shot into a system. Ownership is the payoff. Any edit that makes M2 feel like a set-up-the-tools module steals the compounding feeling; M2's artifact is a system the student wants to keep, not a demo they'll discard.
_trace_note: Re-fire 2026-05-02 round 3. File SHA bdd1557 differs from trace content_sha 834fec9 — file has been edited since the trace was written. Per-phase SHA comparison: phases 1-4 judged structurally stable (module body for Start Here + exercises unchanged); phase 5 (Debrief) SHA 64f1abe — the round-2 fix (omission-specificity forcing function moved inside fenced Debrief prompt, 'Ask once' body sentence removed) is confirmed present in the current file (lines 58-59 inside fence; lines 65-66 observational body retained). All rule verdicts carried from round-2 eval — no new content changes affect any prior verdict. Verdict confirmed PASS. 0 blocking findings, 9 todos.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood contract named and honoured.
Epistemic mood ≠ punitive mood.
Lead with the discipline, not the failure mode.
Strategy-fidelity check — no front-running of downstream teaching moments.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md line 76: Homework section reads 'Read selected sections from What is an Agent on tools, memory, and context.' The compounding lecture (the M2 closer) already resolves 'why plain text' and introduces the three-layer model (sources → memory → rules file). The homework assigns supplementary/what-is-an-agent.md on 'tools, memory, and context' — the same vocabulary — BEFORE M3, which owns the multi-agent architecture teaching moment. Cross-module risk: if what-is-an-agent.md explains tool orchestration or multi-agent patterns in depth, it front-runs M3's felt learning. Requires reading what-is-an-agent.md to confirm scope; flagged as strategy-fidelity risk pending that read.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Confirm what-is-an-agent.md's homework section assignment is scoped to M2-appropriate vocabulary only (persistence, single-agent, memory) and not tool orchestration or multi-agent patterns. If the assigned sections include M3-owned concepts, narrow the homework pointer.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Strategic themes recur in Key Concepts.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md lines 41-44 (Key Concepts): Three bullets — three layers, plain text beats databases, persistence + automation = system. These are tactical. None carry a theme breadcrumb (rule 6 companion). The Agents 101 recurring themes include 'compounding' and 'context shapes output.' The 'context shapes output' throughline (established in M1) is not echoed in Key Concepts. The Compounding lecture names it explicitly ('This is the same mechanism from Module 1: context shapes output, run at system scale') but it doesn't land in the module-level Key Concepts spine.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add one Key Concepts bullet that surfaces the 'context shapes output — now at system scale' throughline from M1, with a breadcrumb like 'Remember: the loop is the product.' Keeps the tactical bullets but adds the strategic echo.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Key Concepts shape — theme breadcrumbs, not a standalone section.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md lines 41-44: All three Key Concepts bullets are plain tactical statements with no bolded breadcrumb. 'Persistence + automation = system. Neither alone is enough.' is the closest to a theme phrasing but is not formatted as a breadcrumb. Per rule 6, 1-3 bullets should carry a short bolded hook.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a bolded breadcrumb to the 'Persistence + automation = system' bullet — e.g., '**Remember: either alone is a toy.**'

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Meta-frame lectures are closers, not openers.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Compounding lecture is the closer — placed after build-your-challenge-memory, before Key Concepts. Correct. Demo: 1st scheduled agent is a ceiling-show opener (shows what's possible before exercises). Correct placement.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Minimal lecturing before contrast-mood exercises.
rules_evaluated.evidence: The pre-exercise material in 'Start here' is three rhetorical questions plus a connector demo (5 min). No named practitioner terms, no framework lecture. The opener arms without teaching. Minimal dosage maintained.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Walk-away test (M2+): student leaves the chair while agent works.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 Beat 3 (memory build) is an explicit walk-away: plan mode approved, Claude writes 5-8 files autonomously. Phase 4 maintenance pass is another. Two walk-away moments in the anchor exercise. Trace phases 3 and 4 confirm agent writes files autonomously.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing functions live in prompts, not body prose.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Fix verified 2026-05-02 round 2, confirmed carried forward in round 3. The Debrief prompt close (lines 58-59 in current file) contains the omission-specificity forcing function inside the fenced block: 'what you chose NOT to include and why. Be specific on the omissions: name at least one session moment where you considered a rule and skipped it, and the reason.' The body prose 'Ask once: what did you choose NOT to write, and why?' sentence has been removed. The surrounding teaching about omissions being the signal is retained in body (lines 65-66) without re-stating the forcing function. Prior REVISE blocker cleared.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Classroom is the default; body teaches the work, trainer manages the room.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Architecture conflict: rule 27 requires authored trainer-cue pause beats in student-visible prose; check_student_facing rule 26 hard-grep bans commanded student actions in body prose. The attempted classroom pause-beat was reverted this cycle because any pause-beat phrasing that names what the student does in the room violates rule 26's equals-not-pupils principle. Approve-with-todo — pause beats scope is the exercise file (build-your-challenge-memory.md), not the module file. Per re-fire context: rule 27 is non-blocking on the module file when its scope is the exercise file.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Fix scoped to build-your-challenge-memory.md exercise file. Pause beats belong there, not in the module file.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Between-module reading appears in two places.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md line 76 (Homework after Module 2): links agent-trigger-list.md, what-is-an-agent.md, personal-agent-homework.md, and module-3-prework.md. Module 3 file would need to be checked to confirm these readings appear in M3's Prework section as well. This is a cross-file verification; flagged as unverified rather than confirmed PASS. Also: the M2 Prework section (line 8) lists module-2-prework.md items but does not list any items from 'Homework after Module 1' (if M1 has between-module readings that should appear here). Symmetry not confirmed.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Verify that all readings listed in 'Homework after Module 2' appear identically in multi-agent-systems.md Prework section. Verify that M2 Prework mirrors any 'Homework after M1' readings from getting-going.md.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Offered structures need a forced-engagement mechanic.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 1 Beat 3: After plan mode runs and Claude writes 5-8 memory files, the student is told 'Claude returns a plan. Read it. Does the topic split match how you actually think about the challenge? If two topics should be one, say so.' This is invitation prose (body), not a forced engagement mechanic. ~90% of students will approve the plan as-is because a 6-item structured plan with good headers reads as competent. No prompt or forced step asks the student to engage critically before approving. The approval gate exists (plan mode) but the prior engagement step ('read it and push back') is only in body prose and will be skipped by speed-runners. Per rule 32: bare approval gates do not produce real reading. NOTE: this finding is scoped to build-your-challenge-memory.md (exercise file), not building-agent-systems.md (module file). Non-blocking on the module file.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Before the plan-mode approval gate, add a short prompt or inline instruction that forces one engagement move: e.g., add to the Beat 3 prompt body 'Before you approve: suggest one topic to merge or one missing topic. Even if the plan looks right, name one thing.' This is a low-cost forcing function that survives the approval-inflation pattern.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: When task selection drives the learning outcome, name floor AND ceiling explicitly.
rules_evaluated.evidence: name-your-challenge.md explicitly names bad examples (done re-org, self-improvement, learning goal) and good examples (pricing redesign, EU market entry, AI strategy). Both floor and ceiling are named. The anti-pattern callout is present and specific.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: No placeholders inside fenced blocks; open-hook only when student input genuinely required.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 3 compound prompt ends with 'New source:' (open-hook colon). This is the correct pattern per rule 1d. However: the action lead-in above the fence reads 'Pick a source that fills a gap: a practitioner article you've been meaning to read, a doc you skipped in Beat 2, an email thread, a local file you can share with Claude. Drop the link, path, or attachment after the colon at the end of the prompt.' This lead-in is technically correct but the instruction 'Drop the link… after the colon' is separated from the colon by the intervening fence — it reads as prose before the fence, not immediately at the cursor position. The student reads the lead-in, then the fenced prompt, and the structural relationship between the lead-in instruction and the colon at the bottom of the fence is easy to miss on first read in a classroom setting. NOTE: this finding is scoped to build-your-challenge-memory.md (exercise file), not building-agent-systems.md (module file). Non-blocking on the module file.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Tighten the action lead-in to a single sentence that names the fill-in clearly: 'Pick one source that fills a gap and paste the URL or path after the colon.' Keep current prose about what counts as a good source but place it before the action lead-in, not between it and the fence.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Every prompt block gets a one-sentence action lead-in.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Beat 2 (ingest): The prompt block is preceded by 'Now Claude pulls the content into sources/. Agent does the heavy lifting; you don't copy-paste.' This is 2 sentences, not one, and neither is a command-verb lead-in summarising what the prompt does. The lead-in should be: 'Ask Claude to pull every source from the curation plan into sources/ using the best method per source type.' Also: the close prompt lead-in 'Run it through the agent you built in Phase 2. That's the one that reads the memory and cites sources.' — two sentences, no command verb, contains an aside. Not a clean action lead-in. NOTE: these findings are scoped to exercise files, not building-agent-systems.md. Non-blocking on the module file.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Audit every prompt block's lead-in for the canonical shape: one sentence, command verb, under 20 words. Three specific fixes: (1) Beat 2 ingest, (2) close prompt, (3) name-your-challenge source-preview.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Canonical presentation shape — lead-in, label with destination, blank line, fenced block.
rules_evaluated.evidence: All prompt blocks in the module and anchor exercise use '**Prompt** *(Claude Code)*' format with no '*(end of prompt)*' closer. Runtime fork spans use the correct rt-code/rt-cowork pattern. No blockquotes or inline backtick wrapping.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Markdown shout inside fenced prompt blocks is dead weight.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Scanning the fenced prompt bodies in the Debrief prompt (lines 52-59 of building-agent-systems.md): no **bold** or *italic* inside the fence. Bullets and numbers used for structure. Path-shaped strings use backticks. Clean.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Runtime-fork prompt blocks: paired divs, shared chip.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md uses rt-code/rt-cowork spans and divs correctly. The Debrief prompt is *(Claude Code)* only (no fork needed — Debrief is runtime-agnostic). Runtime fork patterns in the module header and Start here section are correctly paired. No per-fork chips or forks with different pedagogical content.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Artifact destinations are explicit subpaths, never naked filenames (Agents 101).
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md Debrief prompt (lines 53-59): 'write the first version of CLAUDE.md at the training-directory root' — the instruction does not use the './CLAUDE.md' canonical pattern inside the fence. Without the './' prefix, this is a naked filename in a prose description inside the fence. Per rule 23: deliberate root saves use './' prefix so intent is visible.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Inside the Debrief fenced prompt block, change 'write the first version of CLAUDE.md at the training-directory root' to 'write the first version of ./CLAUDE.md'. The './' makes the intent explicit and harness-testable.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Chain prompts by back-reference and deterministic path.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 2 use-it prompt: 'Read the agent file you just created, apply its role and rules, and use my memory. Ask me for the specific task, then do it.' This relies on Claude inferring which file in agents/ is 'the one you just created.' No filename or path given. If Phase 2 creation produced agents/board-memo-agent.md and the student picks a short name, this works via scrollback. But if another agent exists in agents/ from a prior session, or if the session was cleared, Claude may pick the wrong file or create a new one. Close section also lacks a path for the agent file. NOTE: scoped to build-your-challenge-memory.md exercise file, non-blocking on module file.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a back-reference anchor: 'Read the agent file you just created in agents/ and apply its role' OR instruct the student in the action lead-in to note the filename Claude chose, so the next prompt can reference it by name.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Question-dump default — 'in turn' is too soft for one-at-a-time rhythm.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 2 agent-creation prompt: 'Ask me these one at a time: 1. [job], 2. [rules]...' Uses 'one at a time' phrasing. Debrief prompt in building-agent-systems.md (lines 52-59) is a sequential task prompt, not a question-dump — no rule 15 violation. Non-blocking on module file.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Minimal hand-off + Claude self-augments for send-off prompts.
rules_evaluated.evidence: The Beat 3 memory-build prompt uses plan mode as the control point, not an exhaustive prompt. The prompt gives task + paths + non-negotiable rules (3 rules) — this is close to the minimal hand-off shape. Not a walk-away send-off; Claude asks for approval before acting, which is the right control for a first-time memory build. N/A for strict send-off judgment; design is appropriate for M2's trust-distance level.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood lands: 8+/10 at every beat.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase scores: [8, 8, 8, 9, 8]. Close: 8. No beat below 8. Phase 5 (Debrief) mood re-evaluated against updated Debrief content: fix removed 'Ask once' direction-to-student and moved omission-specificity into the prompt. This change does not alter mood trajectory — the teaching ('What Claude leaves out is often the signal') is retained. Phase 5 mood_score remains 8 (Settled ownership). The 'Compounding rules files are not tidy' maxim present. Close mood_score 8 confirmed.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood doesn't resolve early — M3 unease must not be pre-empted.
rules_evaluated.evidence: M2 closes on 'satisfied compounding.' The Debrief/Compounding lecture does not introduce quality concerns, verification concerns, or multi-agent coordination concerns. The unease that seeds M3 is not touched. Body callout lines 65-66 ('What Claude leaves out is often the signal. A clean summary that names three rules and skips the messy fourth... is the tell. Compounding rules files are not tidy.') teaches pattern-watching of Claude's CLAUDE.md summary — a meta-observation about rules-file writing. It does NOT inject doubt about the memory system itself or the challenge briefings. The closing quote (lines 67-68: 'Your agent is right when it works. Every miss is context you haven't written yet.') is forward-looking and agency-affirming. PASS: callout extends satisfied-compounding rather than eroding it.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Teaching moment lands: the compounding moment (Phase 3 — pages sharpen, claim dropped) reliably triggers across persona skill variation.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 3 is structurally forced: the compound prompt requires Claude to report 'three pages that got sharper (not longer) and one claim that got dropped or replaced.' The reporting mechanic means the teaching moment is observable regardless of how the memory actually changed. The persona who rubber-stamps Phase 3 without reading still has the evidence surface produced by Claude. The moment that could be missed: 'Chat literally cannot do this' (body prose, Phase 3 close). This is in body, not in a prompt, so speed-runners may skip it — but the felt evidence (pages sharpened) is already in Claude's report, and the Compounding lecture names it explicitly. The teaching moment is resilient.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Big Idea fidelity: 'A system remembers, grows, and compounds. Chat doesn't. Your next big challenge is the foundation.'
rules_evaluated.evidence: The Big Idea is honoured. The module's arc — pin the challenge, build the memory, see it compound, write the rules file — is a direct enactment of 'remembers, grows, compounds.' The Compounding lecture's 'Two words, held together' section names persistence + automation as the mechanism. Key Concepts line 3 ('Persistence + automation = system') echoes the Big Idea directly. The 'your next big challenge is the foundation' claim is enacted via the name-your-challenge opener making the challenge selection the first act of the module.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Module-to-module arc: M2 close hands off to M3 open.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md 'Next' section (line 70): 'You have an agent doing real work on your challenge. But the job is getting too big for one agent. What do you split?' This directly seeds M3's Multi-Agent Systems frame. The strategy doc confirms M3's open hook is the question of splitting work across multiple agents. The handoff is explicit and uses M3's vocabulary ('split'). PASS.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Phase boundaries land at conversation-pause beats (classroom default).
rules_evaluated.evidence: Same architecture conflict as check_pedagogy rule 27. Classroom pause-beat attempt reverted this cycle — any student-visible pause-beat phrasing that names what the student does in the room conflicts with check_student_facing rule 26 (hard-grep on commanded student actions). Approve-with-todo pending architecture resolution. The unaddressed risk (45-min exercise with no authored pause) is real but cannot be resolved at this level without an architecture decision on where trainer-cue beats live. Non-blocking on the module file per re-fire scope.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing function in prompts, not body prose (story-class version of check_pedagogy rule 16).
rules_evaluated.evidence: Fix verified 2026-05-02 round 2, confirmed carried forward in round 3. Current file lines 58-59 (inside fence): 'what you chose NOT to include and why. Be specific on the omissions: name at least one session moment where you considered a rule and skipped it, and the reason.' The 'Ask once' body-prose directive has been removed. Body teaching retained (lines 65-66: 'What Claude leaves out is often the signal. A clean summary that names three rules and skips the messy fourth... is the tell. Compounding rules files are not tidy.') — this is observational teaching, not a student action directive. Prior REVISE blocker cleared.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: M2 density check — strategy doc open TODO: 'Module 2 one-big-idea density.'
rules_evaluated.evidence: The content-strategy.md open TODO reads: 'Module 2 one-big-idea density. Stated big idea: a system remembers, grows, and compounds. Module 2 actually teaches persistence, custom-agent-as-markdown, plan mode, three-layer architecture, self-maintenance, crux naming. Six moves. Some hang on the big idea; some are orthogonal.' The module file confirms this: LOs list Build (memory), Construct (agent), Use (plan mode), Analyze (three layers), Evaluate (compounding vs. growing). Five distinct LOs, not all tightly under one Big Idea. Plan mode is the clearest orthogonal: it's a platform feature, not a compounding concept. The Big Idea doesn't cover 'Use plan mode.' The Debrief crux-naming is also not covered by the Big Idea.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: This is the strategy doc's own identified risk. From the story judge's lens: plan mode teaches a control mechanism (student agency over multi-file writes), not compounding itself. If plan mode stays in M2, it needs a tighter Big Idea or a reframing that folds it into the compounding concept. Alternatively: relocate plan mode to M3 or prework as a mechanics primer, keeping M2 purely on persistence + automation + compounding.
